content.problemStatement:
Alice and Bob play a game on a array AAA of size NNN. Alice moves first and their turns alternate.

Alice also has with her another array BBB, initially empty.

On Alice's turn, she chooses any element that remains in AAA, deletes it from AAA, and **appends** it to BBB.

On Bob's turn, he chooses any element that remains in AAA and deletes it.

This proceeds till AAA is empty.

Alice must ensure that BBB always remains sorted — that is, Bi≥Bi−1B_i \ge B_{i - 1}Bi​≥Bi−1​ must hold after all her moves are done.

You're also given a parameter SSS, that is either 000 or 111.

If S=1S = 1S=1, Alice has the further option to skip her turn as many times as she likes (note that Bob isn't allowed to skip his turn).

Alice's score is defined as follows:


If Alice is unable to make a move at some point of time, her score is 000.

Note that this can only happen when S=0S = 0S=0 but appending any element from AAA to BBB would cause it to not be sorted anymore.
Otherwise, if AAA is empty in the end, Alice's score is the sum of the elements in BBB.
Alice's goal is to maximize her score, and Bob's goal is to minimize her score.

If they both play optimally, find Alice's score.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain two space-separated integers TTT and SSS, denoting the number of test cases and the parameter SSS.
Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains one integer NNN, the size of the array.
The second line contains NNN integers, A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​, the array AAA.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line Alice's score under optimal play.
constraints: The sum of NNN over all test cases won't exceed 3⋅1053 \cdot 10^53⋅105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: Alice chooses 222, then Bob chooses 111 on his turn. Alice's final sequence is [2][2][2], which is sorted.
Test case 222: Alice first chooses 111, then Bob chooses 101010, then Alice chooses 222. Alice's final sequence is [1,2][1, 2][1,2], which is sorted.
Had Alice tried to go for 101010 on her first move, her sequence would not be sorted. (Note that Alice is forced to move because S=0S = 0S=0, and can't choose to skip her turn instead)
Test case 333: Alice always ends up with [1,1][1, 1][1,1].

Explanation:
The optimal approaches for Alice remain the same in the first and third test cases even when S=1S = 1S=1.
In the 222nd test case, Alice can now choose 101010 on her first move.
Whatever Bob chooses, Alice skips her next turn; and Bob chooses the last element.
Thus she ends up with a score of 101010.
